front: What is the formal definition of a linear transformation (linear mapping) between two vector spaces?
back: A function T: V → W between two vector spaces V and W over the same field F is a linear transformation if it satisfies two properties for all vectors u, v in V and all scalars c in F: 1) Additivity: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v). 2) Homogeneity: T(cu) = cT(u).

front: What is the kernel (or null space) of a linear transformation T: V → W? What is its key property?
back: The kernel of T is the set of all vectors in V that map to the zero vector in W: ker(T) = { v in V | T(v) = 0 }. A key property is that the kernel is always a subspace of the domain V.

front: What is the image (or range) of a linear transformation T: V → W? What is its key property?
back: The image of T is the set of all vectors in W that are the image of some vector in V: im(T) = { T(v) in W | v in V }. A key property is that the image is always a subspace of the codomain W.

front: State the fundamental Rank-Nullity Theorem for a linear transformation T: V → W, where V is finite-dimensional.
back: The theorem states: dim(V) = rank(T) + nullity(T). Where rank(T) = dim(im(T)) is the dimension of the image, and nullity(T) = dim(ker(T)) is the dimension of the kernel.

front: How is the matrix representation of a linear transformation T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ (with respect to the standard bases) constructed?
back: The standard matrix A for T is an m×n matrix where the j-th column is the vector T(e_j), the image of the j-th standard basis vector e_j of Rⁿ. So, A = [ T(e₁) | T(e₂) | ... | T(e_n) ].
